reasoning:
To resolve YES, Russia must maintain its current grip on five specified cities AND capture at least two of the following major cities by January 1, 2026: Mykolaiv, Zaporizhzhia, Kharkiv, Odesa, Lozova, Sumy, or Chernihiv. All of these target cities require significant territorial advances (tens to hundreds of kilometers) beyond the current frontlines. Historical dynamics of this war show slow, highly attritional advances, with frontlines often moving mere kilometers per month. Taking two major urban centers in a 9-month window would require a complete, cascading collapse of the Ukrainian Armed Forces. 

Recent geopolitical developments further diminish this already low probability. As of March 2025, the US under the Trump administration has re-committed to supplying $2 billion per month in weaponry and sharing intelligence as part of a ceasefire negotiation framework. The reinvigoration of US support significantly bolsters Ukrainian defensive lines and effectively removes the near-term tail risk of a total sudden collapse. Furthermore, if a ceasefire takes effect, it would freeze the lines, making major captures impossible prior to 2026. Therefore, a Russian breakthrough deep enough to capture two of these heavily-fortified administrative centers in the remaining year is extremely implausible. The probability is set to the lowest bound recommended.
